Likkutei Sichos · Infographic
Shavuos 5718 · 1958
A Sicha of the Lubavitcher Rebbe

The Angels' Claim — and the right of first refusal they thought they had.

When Moshe ascended Sinai, the malachim protested: "Give Your splendor upon the heavens." The Sefarim read their protest as a legal claim — the law of dina d'bar metzra, the neighbor's right of pre-emption. The Rebbe walks through the meforshim's five answers, finds each of them lacking, and offers the answer he believes is actually encoded in Moshe's reply.

From a sicha delivered on the second day of Chag HaShavuos, 5718
i.
The Gemara · the angels protest § 1 · Shabbos 88b

"A treasured hidden delight" — and the angels want it back.

The Gemara stages the scene as a courtroom. The angels make a claim; Moshe, prompted by Hashem, must answer them.

3
Talmud Bavli · Mesechta Shabbos
When Moshe ascended to the heights
The Angels
רִבּוֹנוֹ שֶׁל עוֹלָם, מַה לִּילוּד אִשָּׁה בֵּינֵינוּ? . . חֲמוּדָה גְּנוּזָה שֶׁגְּנוּזָה לְךָ . . אַתָּה מְבַקֵּשׁ לִיתְּנָהּ לְבָשָׂר וָדָם . . תְּנָה הוֹדְךָ עַל הַשָּׁמַיִם!

"Master of the Universe — what is one born of woman doing among us? A treasured delight that has been hidden away with You for nine hundred and seventy-four generations — and You wish to give it to flesh and blood? Place Your splendor upon the heavens."

The Holy One, to Moshe
הַחֲזִיר לָהֶן תְּשׁוּבָה.

"Return them an answer."

Moshe Rabbeinu, in answer
לְמִצְרַיִם יְרַדְתֶּם? . . כְּלוּם אַתֶּם עוֹשִׂים מְלָאכָה? . . מַשָּׂא וּמַתָּן יֵשׁ בֵּינֵיכֶם? . . אָב וָאֵם יֵשׁ לָכֶם? . . קִנְאָה יֵשׁ בֵּינֵיכֶם? יֵצֶר הָרָע יֵשׁ בֵּינֵיכֶם?

"Did you descend to Egypt? Do you do labor? Is there commerce among you? Do you have a father and a mother? Is there jealousy among you? A yetzer hara among you?" — and the angels conceded.

ii.
The legal frame the Sefarim discover Bava Metzia 108a · Shulchan Aruch CM 175

A neighbor has the right to take a field back from a buyer.

Dina d'bar metzra: when a person sells a field, the abutting neighbor — the bar metzra — may evict the buyer and take it himself. The Torah's general directive "ועשית הישר והטוב" is given concrete shape here: it is good for the neighbor to hold his fields together. The Sefarim read the angels' protest as exactly this legal claim.

The bar metzra has standing because adjacency itself is a kind of prior claim. The Torah authorizes him to undo the sale — even after it is complete.
The Sefarim's reading of the angels' protest

The Torah is "chamudah genuzah" — hidden in heaven. The angels are in heaven. They are therefore the bar metzra — and they have the right to evict the buyer. "Place Your splendor upon the heavens" is the formal motion.

iii.
Five answers the meforshim give § 2 · Shtei Yados, Pnei David, Mearchei Lev, et al.

Five ways to defeat the angels' claim — on the law's own terms.

Each works by locating an exemption inside the law of dina d'bar metzra itself. Each is plausible. None of them — the Rebbe will argue — actually appears in Moshe's words.

i.
Wrong category

Bar metzra applies only to land — not movable property.

A field can't be replicated next door. A chair, a coin, a scroll — these are mitaltelin, and there is no pre-emption right over them. Torah, the meforshim say, isn't land.

קרקע / מטלטלין · land vs. movable property
ii.
Wrong transaction

Bar metzra applies to a sale — but Torah was given as a gift.

The right of pre-emption attaches to mechirah, not to matanah. Chazal explicitly call the Torah matanah tovah — "three good gifts the Holy One gave Israel." A gift has no neighbor.

מתנה · gift, not sale
iii.
Family exemption

Israel are relatives to the Giver — and to family, no pre-emption.

"Which great nation has G-d so near to it" — krovim eilav. More: Israel are banim laMakom, children. The law makes an exception for relatives and especially for children — they sit closer than any bar metzra.

בנים למקום · "Children to the Place"
iv.
Moshe is the neighbor

Moshe is ish ha-Elokim — half man, half Above.

The Midrash on "the man of G-d" reads: "from his middle down a man, from his middle up — Elokim." Moshe himself shares the angels' upper region. He is a bar metzra. The angels and Moshe are co-neighbors; the field belongs to whoever takes it first.

איש האלקים · "the man of G-d"
v.
Partner exemption

Moshe is a partner with Hashem — "as if he became a partner in the act of Creation."

The verse describes Moshe "judging the people from morning to evening." Chazal: a judge of true justice is "as if he became a partner with the Holy One in maaseh bereishis." And the law is explicit — if the buyer is a partner of the seller, dina d'bar metzra does not apply. The angels are outside the partnership.

שותף להקב"ה במעשה בראשית · the partner exemption
iv.
And two patches, for the last three § 2 · contd.

But Moshe didn't receive Torah for himself alone.

The last three answers depend on something special about Moshe — his being a relative, half-Above, or a partner. But the Torah was given for all of Israel. The meforshim therefore add two further answers that extend the same logic to every Jew:

a · All Israel as bnei metzra

Every Jewish soul is גְּזוּרָה from beneath the Throne of Glory.

Each neshamah originates "in heaven," carved from beneath the Kisei HaKavod. So every Jew, not only Moshe, is a bar metzra to the Torah that sat above.

Souls — like Torah — are gnuzos in shamayim.
b · All Israel as partners

Before Sinai, Israel already had שַׁבָּת — given at Marah.

Chazal: "Whoever recites vayechulu on Friday night becomes a partner with Hashem in the act of creation." Having already received Shabbos before Matan Torah, every Jew was a shutaf — and the partner exemption applies to all of them.

"Va'yechulu" = partnership in maaseh bereishis.
v.
The fundamental problem § 3–4 · "Ikar chaser min ha-sefer"

The most important thing is missing from the book.

All five answers are clever — but not one of them is hinted at in what Moshe actually said. Moshe did not say: "Torah is movable property." He did not say: "Israel are Your children." He did not say: "I'm Your partner."

He said the opposite. Look at the words.

What Moshe Actually Said
לְמִצְרַיִם יְרַדְתֶּם?

Did you descend to Egypt?

עוֹשִׂים מְלָאכָה?

Do you do labor?

מַשָּׂא וּמַתָּן יֵשׁ?

Is there commerce among you?

אָב וָאֵם יֵשׁ?

Father and mother?

קִנְאָה · יֵצֶר הָרָע?

Jealousy? Yetzer hara?

Every one of Moshe's points pulls in the same direction — and it is the opposite direction from the meforshim's answers.

The meforshim said: Israel are close, related, partners, half-Above. Moshe said: Israel went down to Egypt. Israel work. Israel buy and sell. Israel have parents, jealousy, a yetzer hara. He was not arguing his case from elevation. He was arguing it from descent.

So either Moshe gave a different answer than the meforshim are giving — or the meforshim are reading something else into a text that doesn't carry it. The Rebbe sides with the first option: Moshe's words must contain his real answer.

The Rebbe's principle

If an answer is real, it should be visible in the words of the one giving it.

When Moshe's response and the meforshim's answers point in opposite directions, the response wins.

§ 3 · "Ikar chaser min ha-sefer"
vi.
One more attempt — and why it also fails § 5 · The sod / dchika lei shaata pair

Maybe the angels only wanted the inner Torah — and lost on a technicality?

A more sophisticated reading: angels know they have no portion in the peshat of Torah, which is about action in a body. They were only suing for the sod. Then two further laws step in:

a · The "sold-it-all" exemption

Israel received the whole Torah — sod included.

The law: "If one sells all his property to a single buyer, dina d'bar metzra does not apply." Once the entire estate moves as one transaction, the neighbor cannot carve off a fragment. Israel received pshat, remez, drush and sod — one indivisible package.

"Machar kol nechasav le-echad."
b · The "urgency" exemption

Israel needed the Torah; the angels only wanted to expand.

A further rule: where the bar metzra wants the field only leharvachah (to expand his holdings) and the buyer is dchika lei shaata (his time is pressing) — the buyer wins. Torah is the life of a Jew; without it the yetzer prevails. For angels, with no yetzer to overcome, sod was a luxury.

"Dchika lei shaata."
But — same problem.

If Moshe's answer were "you sold the whole estate" or "Israel needs it more," we would expect him to say so. Instead his elaboration — "labor, commerce, father and mother" — points to a different argument entirely. Not technical defeat. Something about the nature of Torah and the nature of the world Torah is going to.

vii.
The Rebbe's answer § 6 · Yishuv adif · dirah b'tachtonim

There is one more exemption — and it is the one Moshe was using.

The Shulchan Aruch gives the case explicitly. The neighbor wants the field for planting. The buyer wants it to build a house on. Settlement — yishuv — wins. The pre-emption right is overridden.

A house — a place lived in — outranks a field. When the buyer's purpose is to settle the land, the bar metzra's pre-emption right is overridden.
Why Torah was given
נִתְאַוָּה הַקָּדוֹשׁ בָּרוּךְ הוּא לִהְיוֹת לוֹ דִּירָה בַּתַּחְתּוֹנִים

"The Holy One longed for a dwelling in the lower worlds." Just as a person inhabits a dwelling with his full essence, so Hashem desired that His Essence Itself be drawn down into the lower world. Torah is the instrument by which the dwelling is built.

Tanchuma Naso 16 · Tanya ch. 36

This collapses the entire case. The angels asked: are we not the bar metzra to a treasure stored in heaven? — and the answer is: this transaction is for building a dwelling. The buyer's purpose is yishuv. Pre-emption does not apply.

And now Moshe's words land. "To Egypt you descended. You do labor. You buy and sell. You have parents, jealousy, a yetzer hara." He is not citing weakness; he is naming the address. The dwelling is being built there — in a world with parents and commerce and a yetzer. The Torah is being given to the place where the house is going up.

The meforshim were searching for an exemption inside the law. The Rebbe finds it. But the exemption is not "Israel are close to G-d" — it is "yishuv adif," and the settlement is the tachton.

The Rebbe's reading

Torah is for the lower world — not in spite of its lowness, but because of it.

§ 6 · The hinge of the sicha
viii.
Why specifically the lowest § 7 · The lever mashal

To lift the building — place the lever beneath the lowest stone.

An obvious objection: the angels' world also needs the divine Essence drawn down into it. Even the highest spiritual worlds receive only a radiance — they are themselves a yeridah from Hashem's face. Why couldn't the Torah be given there, and the dwelling be built there?

The Rebbe answers with a mashal. A building cannot be lifted from its middle. The lever must go under the lowest part.

If you put the lever halfway up, the lower portion stays on the ground. To lift the entire building you must reach under the lowest stone. That is why Torah was given to the world "than which there is none lower."

And so Moshe was not apologizing for "labor, commerce, jealousy, yetzer hara." He was pointing to them. Of course the angels' world also needs the dwelling. The way to build it for them, too, is to begin at the bottom.

The summary of § 7

Torah must be given specifically in "a world than which there is no lower" — full of klipos, jealousy, yetzer hara. Only from there can the whole structure of seder hahishtalshelus be lifted into a dwelling for Hashem's Essence.

ix.
The deepest layer § 8 · Av v'em yesh lachem

Even angels cannot make a dwelling — because only Atzmus can make something from nothing.

A last objection. Yes, Torah must be given below to do its work in the lower world. But could the angels not also build their own kind of dwelling, "above," through their own avodah?

No. The Rebbe gives the reason: a dirah l'Atzmuso cannot be produced from above at all. The Essence is drawn down only through one specific power — the power of hischadshus, of making something genuinely new from nothing. That power lives only in Atzmus itself, and it acts only through a soul fighting through gevulim in the lowest world.

Hischadshus — making yesh from ayin — is the signature of Atzmus alone. Holadah is the human mirror: a new being where there was none. Angels, who exist only as emanation, do not have access to this power.

This is why Moshe's question "av v'em yesh lachem?" — do you have parents? — is not a throwaway. It is the crown of his argument. To have parents is to be a yesh that came from an ayin. To have parents is to have, in your very being, a trace of hischadshus.

And the work of a Jew in this world — the iskafia and is'hapcha of klipah, taking what is "literally against G-d" and turning it into kedushah — is the same act on a different plane. It is making a yesh into an ayin, refining matter into something it could not otherwise have been. It is therefore an act only possible to one whose own being already carries the imprint of Atzmus.

The neshamah, descending into the gevulim of this world, becomes molid — she takes a tachton and produces from it (and through it, from all of seder hahishtalshelus) a dwelling for the Atzmus and Mahus of Hashem.

The full argument

If a dwelling for Atzmus required no hischadshus, the angels could build it.

It does require hischadshus.

Hischadshus is in Atzmus alone, and acts only through one who is itself a "yesh from ayin."

Angels have no av v'em. Israel does.

§ 8 · the last move
x.
The Sicha, in summary Synthesis

One claim, one exemption, one purpose.

i.
The Angels

"We are the bar metzra."

Torah is hidden in heaven; we live in heaven; the field is ours by right of pre-emption.

ii.
The Meforshim

Find an exemption — any exemption.

Movable property. Gift. Children. Half-Above. Partner. Each works — but none appears in Moshe's actual words.

iii.
Moshe's Real Answer

Yishuv adif.

The buyer is building a dwelling — dirah b'tachtonim. Pre-emption gives way to settlement. The destination is the lowest world precisely.

iv.
And the deepest reason

"Father and mother."

Only a being with parents — with hischadshus in its very being — can make a dwelling for Atzmus. Angels cannot.

The verse that closes the argument
אוֹתִי אַתֶּם לוֹקְחִים
osi atem lokchim

"It is Me you are taking." Through the Torah — given below, to a world with parents and labor and jealousy and a yetzer — Hashem Himself is drawn into the dwelling. The angels lost the case because they were never bidding on the same thing.

Likkutei Sichos משיחת יום ב' דחג השבועות תשי"ח
